title: How to Use OpenCode for AI-Assisted Python Coding
content: — FREE Email Series — 🐍 Python Tricks 💌  🔒 No spam. Unsubscribe any time. Table of Contents Table of Contents OpenCode is an open-source AI coding agent that runs in your terminal and lets you analyze and refactor a Python project through conversational commands. In this guide, you’ll install it on your system, set it up with a free Google Gemini API key, and learn the basics of how to use it in your daily programming work. Here’s what OpenCode’s main interface looks like: OpenCodeworks as a conversational assistant you explicitly direct. Ask it to analyze functions, refactor code, or explain issues. PressEnterto send your query, and you’ll get a response with full awareness of your project context. It supports more than seventy-five AI providers, including Anthropic, OpenAI, andGoogle Gemini. If you’re a Python developer who prefers working in the terminal, OpenCode offers deliberate, context-aware assistance and a customizableAGENTS.mdconfiguration file. Take the Quiz:Test your knowledge with our interactive “How to Use OpenCode for AI-Assisted Python Coding” quiz. You’ll receive a score upon completion to help you track your learning progress: Interactive Quiz Quiz yourself on OpenCode: install it, connect an AI provider, and use it to analyze and refactor Python from your terminal. Before you start working with OpenCode, you’ll need to fulfill the following prerequisites regarding your current system and working environment: You also need anAI provider account. In this guide, you’ll useGoogle AI Studioto get a free GeminiAPIkey. The free Gemini tier lets you follow along without any additional costs. However, you can also use Anthropic, OpenAI, orGitHub Copilotif you already have subscriptions to those services. This guide uses a sample project consisting of a dice-rolling script. You’ll find the fullsource codein a collapsible block at the start of Step 2. The download below includes the starting script and the final refactored version so you can compare your work when you’re done: Get Your Code:Click here to download the free sample codeyou’ll use to learn about AI-assisted Python coding with OpenCode. You’ll also need some background knowledge ofPython programmingand basic experience with your operating system’sterminal or command line. It’s time to install OpenCode and get it talking to a model. You’ll install the tool on your system, authenticate with Gemini using a free API key, configure a default model, and verify that OpenCode responds correctly to your Python questions before you start coding with it. The quickest way to install OpenCode is to use the official installation script, which you can do with the following command: This script detects your platform, downloads the appropriate binary, installs the tool, and adds it to yourPATH. If you prefer a package manager, you can also install OpenCode with Homebrew on macOS or Linux: Note that the Homebrew team maintains the official formula and updates it less frequently than the installation script above. Alternatively, you can install it as aNode.jspackage usingnpmif you already have this tool on your system: If you’re onWindows, the best experience comes from using WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux). Set up WSL first by followingMicrosoft’s WSL installation guide, then open a WSL terminal and run thecurlcommand above. For optimal performance, you should store your project within the WSL filesystem rather than on a Windows drive. Note:To learn how to set up a Windows machine for working with Python, check outYour Python Coding Environment on Windows: Setup Guide. After installation, you can verify whether OpenCode is working by running the following command: If this command outputs a version number like1.14.20, then the tool is ready to use. In contrast, if the command displays an error message, then you can try reloading your shell settings and check the version again: If you get an error again, then you may need to manually add OpenCode to your systemPATH. If that’s the case, run the following commands to solve the issue: Once theopencode --versioncommand succeeds, you can start OpenCode by running the following command in any directory: This command will launch the OpenCode interface in your terminal window. Now, you’re ready to connect with an AI provider and configure the model. Once OpenCode’sTUI (text-based user interface)loads, you can connect with Google Gemini, which is the AI provider you’ll use in this tutorial. Type/connectand pressEnter. A list of supported providers appears on your screen. Use the arrow keys to navigate toGoogleand pressEnter. You’ll then be prompted for an API key. Google AI Studio generates Gemini API keys in a few clicks. Here are the steps: If you’ve used Google AI Studio before, the steps may differ slightly. You may need to create or import a Google Cloud Project, then clickCreate API keyto generate the key. Note:The credentials are stored at~/.local/share/opencode/auth.jsonand won’t be requested again unless you want to add or change providers. Then, select the default model,Gemini 3 Flash Preview, which offers a good balance of speed and capability for day-to-day coding tasks. You can change the model at any time by typing/modelsand pressingEnter. You’ll also be prompted about the modelvariant, which refers to the thinking effort. Choose the default to get started. To test the setup, go ahead and ask a Python question: What’s the difference between list.append() and list.extend() in Python? After a few seconds, you’ll get an explanation of the differences. If everything works as expected, you’re ready to work with a Python project. Before moving on, it’s worth getting familiar with the TUI. In the bottom right corner, you’ll see a few tips with keyboard shortcuts. For example, if you pressCtrl+T, then you’ll move between the model variants:default,low, andhigh. If you pressTab, then you’ll change between thePlanandBuildmodes, which you’ll touch on in the next section. Note:Even though you can manually select the variant, it’s a good idea to use theDefaultvariant because it automatically adjusts the thinking effort to the task complexity. This behavior avoidsoverthinking, which is when you spendtokensand time on simple tasks. It also avoidsunderthinking, which is when difficult tasks fail because the model didn’t think enough. In theDefaultvariant, you’ll see how OpenCode cycles throughdefault(invisible),low, andhighvariants automatically. PressingCtrl+Pgives you access to the list of available commands. Go ahead and explore the list to get familiar with the commands you can use during your OpenCode sessions. OpenCode also gives you a few shortcuts for steering a session: Type/in the input box to see the full list of slash commands. Here’s a summary of some useful ones: With this quick setup and walkthrough of OpenCode, you’re ready to put it to work on a practical Python project. In this step, you’ll initialize OpenCode in a sample Python project, create anAGENTS.mdconfiguration file with Python-specific guidelines, and use the tool to analyze and improve code with full project context. You’ll also see howPlanandBuildmodes complement each other during a typical refactoring session. The sample project is a dice-rolling script from theBuild a Dice-Rolling Application With Pythontutorial. Start by creating a directory for the project and navigating into it: Then, click theShow/Hidetoggle, copy the sample code, and save it in thedice.pyfile: Sample Code for dice.pyShow/Hide The dice-rolling project works as follows: With everything in place, launch OpenCode from the project directory: Once it loads, run the initialization slash command while you’re inBuildmode: /init This command analyzes your entire project structure, examining file names, imports, directory layout, and existing documentation. Then, it generates anAGENTS.mdfile in the project root. This file contains guidelines that help it understand your project’s architecture, dependencies, and coding patterns. Again, you can click theShow/Hidetoggle to see what the file might look like: AGENTS.md FileShow/Hide In this tutorial, you’re using a toy project. However, if you’re working on a team project, then it’s a good practice to commitAGENTS.mdto version control so all contributors get consistent AI assistance. OpenAGENTS.mdin your editor and add some Python-specific guidelines at the end of the file: Save the file and return to OpenCode. To make the tool aware of your changes, you have two options: If you’ve manually modified your project structure and want the agent to updateAGENTS.mdbased on the current code state, then you can run the/initcommand again. With theAGENTS.mdfile in place, you can start interacting with your code. OpenCode operates in two modes. You switch between them with theTabkey, as you’ve already seen: Always switch toPlanmode before asking for significant changes. It’s the equivalent of reviewing a diff before applying it. You can reference a specific file in any prompt using the@syntax. Switch toPlanmode withTab, then ask for an analysis of yourdice.pyfile: Analyze @dice.py for potential issues, bugs, and code quality problems The tool will identify issues based on the guidelines in yourAGENTS.mdfile. It might identify things like the following: At the end of the report, you’ll find a proposed plan, which may look something like the following: Proposed Plan If you would like me to address these, I suggest the following refactoring steps: Would you like me to proceed with these changes? If you’re satisfied with the plan, pressTabto switch back toBuildmode and confirm. OpenCode will then apply the changes to the file. Review the changes in your favorite code editor orIDEand runpython dice.pyto verify the script still works after applying them. Click theShow/Hidetoggle to check the resulting code. Note that this code will certainly differ from what you’ll get: Improved Code for the Dice-Rolling ScriptShow/Hide Here’s a breakdown of the main changes: If only some proposed changes look good to you, ask the agent to refine the plan. Alternatively, you can ask the agent to apply specific items in the plan. This could be a better approach if the changes are considerable. If you apply changes step by step and the last change doesn’t look right or good enough, run/undoto revert it and start again with a fine-tuned prompt. You can run/undorepeatedly to walk back through previous messages one at a time. That’s it! This is a typical workflow for using OpenCode as a coding assistant for your Python projects. Since OpenCode allows conversational interaction, you can ask the agent to make whatever change you like in your codebase. After each section, it’s also good practice to run the/initcommand and have the agent polish or update theAGENTS.mdfile for future coding sessions. In this section, you’ll explore three features that set OpenCode apart from other AI coding tools: OpenCode supports more than seventy-five AI providers. You can connect to any of these providers at any time by running the/connectslash command and setting up the corresponding API key when required. Once you’ve added a provider, you can switch between models on the fly with the/modelscommand. A picker lists all configured models. Select one, and your next message will go to that model. You don’t need to restart the session. Try running the same analysis prompt against two different models to compare response quality and find the best fit for different types of tasks. OpenCode uses theLanguage Server Protocol (LSP)to interact with your code. It ships with more than thirty built-inlanguage serversthat run in the background when you open a source code file. For Python coding, the Pyright server is the default and provides real-time diagnostics, including type errors, undefined names, and import problems. OpenCode passes this information directly to the model, so when you ask it to analyze your code, it already has the same diagnostic information that your editor would surface. If you prefer agraphical interface, start the web UI from a separate terminal: This command launches a local server on127.0.0.1and opens your default browser to the same conversational interface as the TUI: Once the web UI is ready, you can click theOpen projectbutton and select yourdice-project/folder. Go ahead and explore the interface. You’ll find many useful features. Note that you can run a terminal session and the web interface simultaneously in the same project. Both interfaces will share the same sessions and state. Theweb interfaceis handy when you want a larger display for reviewing longer responses, or when you’re sharing your screen and want something more accessible than a terminal. You’ve installed OpenCode, authenticated with your Google account to use Gemini models, and used it to analyze and refactor a Python project from your terminal. You also created theAGENTS.mdconfiguration file, which gives the agent persistent context about your project. InPlanmode, you reviewed changes before they were applied. With model switching, built-in LSP diagnostics, and the web interface, you have a complete AI coding workflow without leaving your development environment. In this tutorial, you’ve learned how to: With these skills, you can bring AI-assisted coding into your Python projects without leaving the terminal or relying on a paid subscription. Now that you have OpenCode up and running, here are some directions to explore next: If you want a structured curriculum for AI-assisted Python development, check out Real Python’sPython Coding With AI learning path. It organizes resources on tools likeClaude Code,Cursor, and theGemini CLIinto one path you can work through at your own pace. Get Your Code:Click here to download the free sample codeyou’ll use to learn about AI-assisted Python coding with OpenCode. Now that you have some experience with OpenCode in Python, you can use the questions and answers below to check your understanding and recap what you’ve learned. These FAQs are related to the most important concepts you’ve covered in this tutorial. Click theShow/Hidetoggle beside each question to reveal the answer. Is OpenCode free to use?Show/Hide Yes, it’s free and open source. The software itself has no cost, and you can connect it to Google Gemini’s free tier to use it without any subscription fees. Paid AI providers like Anthropic or OpenAI can also be connected if you prefer their models. What isAGENTS.mdin OpenCode?Show/Hide AGENTS.mdis a project configuration file read at the start of every session. It contains guidelines about your project’s architecture, coding style, and conventions, giving the AI consistent context across all your interactions. Run/initto have OpenCode generate it automatically from your project structure. What is the difference between thePlanandBuildmodes in OpenCode?Show/Hide ThePlanmode proposes changes and explains the approach without modifying any files, while theBuildmode applies changes directly to your project. Switch between them with theTabkey. ThePlanmode is useful for reviewing a strategy before committing to significant code changes. How do you reference a specific file in an OpenCode prompt?Show/Hide Type@in the OpenCode prompt to open a fuzzy file picker. Start typing a partial filename to narrow the results, then select the file to add it as context. The file’s contents become visible to the AI for that prompt.
